title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a neighbourhood arts festival. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Julia, The Street Parade Marshal
Julia volunteered as a parade marshal after her dance group agreed to perform at the festival. [47]She had imagined waving cheerfully at the crowd, but most of her work involved keeping performers moving through narrow streets without gaps opening between groups.[/47] Her experience leading walking tours helped her speak loudly and calmly while still smiling. [48]She marked waiting points in chalk the night before, so each group knew exactly where to gather before joining the route.[/48] The most difficult moment came when a delivery van blocked the corner just as the drummers were due to turn into the market square. Julia improvised a pause, and the dancers filled it so well that spectators thought it was planned. [55]She says a parade looks spontaneous only when many people quietly prevent confusion.[/55] She now keeps safety pins, water and a street map in every festival bag, along with plasters for performers wearing new shoes.
texts.B:
### Karim, The Sound Tent Assistant
Karim helped in the sound tent because he played guitar and wanted to understand live events better from the other side of the stage. [49]He quickly learned that the audience notices silence between acts more than small mistakes during a song.[/49] His job was to label cables, check microphones, move stands and warn performers when their setup time was nearly over. [50]The worst pressure came when a poet whispered into the microphone and blamed the speakers, although she was standing too far from it.[/50] Karim had to explain the problem kindly without embarrassing her in front of the crowd. The festival taught him that technical work includes diplomacy, not just volume knobs, batteries and neat rows of switches. He now respects sound engineers far more than before, because they solve problems everyone hears but few people understand. He also carries gaffer tape like treasure, wrapped around an old pencil in his pocket.
texts.C:
### Nerea, The Children's Art Tent Leader
Nerea ran the children's art tent, where families decorated paper lanterns for the evening display beside the river during the final procession. [51]She discovered that the safest materials were not always the easiest to use, especially when glue sticks dried out faster than expected.[/51] As a primary teacher, she was used to controlled mess, but the open tent brought wind, grandparents, toddlers and spilled paint into every plan. [52]She introduced a drying line with numbered pegs after several children accidentally took home someone else's lantern.[/52] Her favourite moment was seeing older children help younger ones thread handles through the paper. Nerea says the tent worked best when instructions were simple enough to survive excitement, noise and the temptation to run around. She judged success not by neat lanterns, but by whether children carried them away proudly, even when they were wrinkled, patched or wildly uneven and shedding glitter across the grass.
texts.D:
### Victor, The Artist Liaison
Victor volunteered as artist liaison because he knew many local musicians and painters from previous community projects and open-mic nights. [53]He had to collect precise arrival times, individual access needs and payment forms without making creative people feel treated like paperwork.[/53] His previous job in a travel agency helped when a performer missed a train and three workshops had to be rearranged before lunch. [54]The most sensitive task was finding a quiet room for an illustrator who became overwhelmed by the noise of the main square.[/54] Victor realised that looking after artists meant protecting their ability to perform or teach, not flattering them. He was surprised by how many problems could be solved by a phone charger, clear directions, a sandwich or five minutes alone behind a closed door. [56]He now writes shorter emails, because festival week taught him that tired people do not read long instructions, however beautifully formatted.[/56]

questions.question: found that keeping the procession evenly spaced was a central part of the role?
questions.explanation: Julia had to keep performers moving without gaps opening between groups.

questions.question: prepared visible ground markings so groups knew where to wait?
questions.explanation: Julia marked waiting points in chalk before the parade.

questions.question: learned that pauses between performances could be more noticeable than errors during them?
questions.explanation: Karim found audiences noticed silence between acts more than small musical mistakes.

questions.question: had to correct a performer's microphone use without causing public embarrassment?
questions.explanation: Karim kindly explained why the poet's microphone was not working well.

questions.question: discovered that safe craft supplies could still create practical difficulties?
questions.explanation: Nerea found safe materials were not always easy to use because glue dried quickly.

questions.question: introduced a numbered system after finished items were confused?
questions.explanation: Nerea used numbered pegs after children took the wrong lanterns.

questions.question: needed to gather administrative details without making artists feel reduced to forms?
questions.explanation: Victor collected details and forms while avoiding making artists feel like paperwork.

questions.question: arranged a calmer space for someone affected by the noisy surroundings?
questions.explanation: Victor found a quiet room for an illustrator overwhelmed by noise.

questions.question: says public spontaneity depends on unseen prevention of disorder?
questions.explanation: Julia says a parade looks spontaneous when people quietly prevent confusion.

questions.question: changed their written communication because tired people ignore lengthy instructions?
questions.explanation: Victor now writes shorter emails after learning tired people do not read long instructions.
